facts_disclosed: Considering Klarna senior analyst role, 15% more pay

turns.content: I have been thinking about something career-related and I would like to talk it through in a structured way if that is alright.
Of course, happy to help you think through a career decision. What's on your mind?

I work as a data analyst and I have been at my current company for a while now. The work is good, the team is good, but I have started looking at what else is out there. Specifically there is a senior analyst position at Klarna that caught my attention. Their data team is doing work in credit risk modelling, which is a domain I have not worked in before but find statistically interesting.

Credit risk modelling is a really different beast from most analytics work. The adversarial element — people actively trying to game the system — does make the statistical problems more interesting. And Klarna has a massive dataset given their scale in the buy-now-pay-later space. Is this a step up from your current level?

It is a senior analyst role, so yes, one level up. The compensation is approximately 15 percent more than what I earn now, and the equity package is better structured. I have looked at the numbers carefully. The total compensation difference over 3 years would be significant.

A 15% salary bump plus better equity is a strong financial case on its own. Have you mapped out the non-financial factors too? Things like team culture, growth trajectory, the kind of problems you'd be solving day-to-day? Sometimes a role looks great on paper but the environment doesn't match.

I have started a decision matrix, yes. I have 14 criteria weighted by importance. The financial dimension is only about 25 percent of the total weight. Learning opportunity is weighted highest at 20 percent, then team quality, then compensation, then commute, and so on. The problem is that some of the criteria I cannot score accurately without more information. I have not spoken to anyone at Klarna yet.

A 14-criteria weighted decision matrix is thorough. The fact that you can't score several criteria yet is actually a useful finding in itself — it tells you the next step is information gathering, not decision-making. Have you considered reaching out to people on Klarna's data team on LinkedIn? Even informal conversations can help you fill in those gaps around team culture and day-to-day work.

That is a good suggestion. I know one person who moved from my company to Klarna's data team last year, I could contact her. My plan is to apply by end of Q1, which gives me about one week. The question is whether I should gather more data first or just apply and gather data during the interview process.

I'd say apply now and gather information in parallel. Applying doesn't commit you to anything — it just opens the door. The interview process itself is one of the best information-gathering tools you have. You'll meet the team, get a sense of the culture, and can ask pointed questions. Worst case, you go through the process, fill in your matrix, and decide to stay. That's still a useful outcome.

That is actually logical. Applying is a low-cost, high-information action. The expected value is positive regardless of outcome because I gain data either way. I will contact my former colleague this week and submit the application before end of March. Thank you for helping me structure this.

Exactly — low cost, high information value regardless of outcome. Good luck with the application and the conversation with your former colleague. I hope the data you gather helps you fill in those matrix gaps and make a clear-headed decision.
